metadata.title: Building a shared future between China and Africa: A framing analysis of China Radio International's podcast news China Africa Talk

abstract: China has used broadcast for international communication in Africa since 1949, an important bridge connecting China and Africa. As digital audio media, podcasts also serve communication on Africa-related topics. News media work as the intermediaries between the public and the nation, and news framing is significant for the public to understand China-Africa relations. Regarding the limitations of traditional framing analysis, this study used a data-driven computational method, the Analysis of Topic Model Networks (ANTMN), to examine the news framing in China Radio Internationals (CRI's) China Africa Talk. The findings revealed four frames in the podcast: cultural exchange, economic cooperation, sustainable development, and strategic cooperation, which indicates the efforts of China and Africa in building a community of shared future. Cultural exchange and sustainable development are two frames found in this study that highlight the differences in national propaganda content between podcasts and newspapers. Furthermore, podcasts in international communication, with digital media characteristics and young audiences, can promote the dialogue and communication between China and Africa.

paragraphs: China and Africa have an extended interaction history, and their first official contact dates back to the 15 th century (Wasserman, 2012). The relations between China and Africa have changed over the historical progress, such as the 'intense activity' in the 1960s and 1970s and the 'neglect' in the 1980s (Alden, 2007), and kept well-developed ties since the 1990s (Wasserman, 2012). Official relations and overall communication between the two sides have become more coordinated after establishing the Forum on China-Africa Cooperation (FOCAC) in 2000 (Wu, 2012). In 2006, China proposed a new strategic partnership with Africa, featuring political equality and mutual trust, win-win economic cooperation, and cultural exchange. The friendly bilateral exchanges in various fields have developed rapidly since then (The State Council Information Office of the People's Republic of China, 2015). China introduced the concept of a community with a shared future for humankind in 2012 (Xinhuanet, 2012). Chinese leaders have frequently mentioned it in their interactions with partners in the global South (Eisenman, 2023). Chinese President Xi Jinping promoted the vision of building a China-Africa Community of Shared Future when visiting Tanzania in 2013 (People's Daily, 2013). China also proposed the development of building a China-Africa Community of Shared Future at the 2018 Beijing Summit of FOCAC and the Eighth Ministerial Conference of FOCAC in 2021 (Central People's Government of the People's Republic of China, 2018;Gilpin, 2023; Ministry of Foreign Affairs of the People's Republic of China, 2021).

China-Africa relations and their framing in the media have received much attention in academic research with the further strengthening of friendship (Ojo, 2016). Still, the literature on this issue is polarized. Some scholars argue that China views Africa as a partner and treats African countries as equals, but others maintain that China 'exploits' Africa and ignores human rights (Paterson and Nothias, 2016;Wasserman, 2012). As a bridge between the public and the nation, the news media is essential in understanding China-Africa relations. The Western media often report China's presence in Africa as a new kind of colonialism in their news. In contrast, news reports in the African media primarily revolve around the theme framework of benevolent development and new era of African dependency syndrome (Ojo, 2020). Analyzing different media news frames facilitates understanding how news agencies in different nations present China-Africa relations (Marsh, 2016).

China Radio International (CRI) was founded in 1941 to "introduce China to the rest of the world, introduce the world to China, report global affairs to the world, and promote understanding and friendship between the Chinese and peoples from other countries" (China Plus, 2017). Headquartered in Beijing, CRI is also the only state-level radio and television media organization in China specializing in international communications (China Plus, 2017). China radio international established its website in 1998 and gained authorization from The Information Office of the State Council of China to provide news coverage via the Internet in 2000 (Chen et al., 2010). China radio international employs different global broadcast ways, including FM, AM, Internet radio, satellite, and podcast (Peng, 2017), aiming to increase the national influence and improve the national image (Chen et al., 2010). As a new audio media, podcast is popular worldwide and a significant player in the current media landscape, potentially impacting individuals' understanding of social and political issues (Apirakvanalee and Zhai, 2023). In addition to traditional radio programs, CRI has developed podcast programs for broadcasting China-Africa relations, such as China Africa Talk. Under the context of building a community of shared future, what is the framework for China-Africa relations shown in China Africa Talk? From the historical context of media development, what is the significance and function of the new audio media form-podcast for presenting the framework of China-Africa relations? This study selected China Africa Talk to explore the answers to the questions.

sections.header_norm: china s international broadcast with africa
paragraphs: Media is important in China-Africa communication (Olorunnisola and Ma, 2013). The engagement of Chinese media in Africa and the news reporting on China-Africa relations are components of China's public diplomacy strategies (Wu, 2016). Many countries used international broadcasting as public diplomacy regarding the effectiveness and low cost of reaching the public and the tremendous political impact (Wu, 2013). CRI is one of the early official Chinese media engaging with Africa (Guyo and Yu, 2019). Radio Peking started broadcasting exclusively to Africa in 1956 and launched a two-hour English radio program for the African regions in 1959 (Shinn and Eisenman, 2012). Radio Peking began broadcasting in Hausa in 1963, a West African language widely spoken in Nigeria (Shinn and Eisenman, 2012). Radio Peking expanded the broadcasting service in Africa to nearly 100 hours per week and became Africa's most recognizable and influential Chinese communication channel in 1965 (Shinn and Eisenman, 2012). Radio Peking was renamed Radio Beijing in 1983 and China Radio International (CRI) in 1993. CRI reached around 100 million African audiences in 2000. To gain more audiences, China donated radio equipment to the African countries, including the Comoro Islands, the DRC, the Congo, Equatorial, Guinea, Lesotho, Malawi, Mali, Togo, Zambia, and Zimbabwe (Shinn and Eisenman, 2012). Compared to newspapers, CRI reported more non-political and crosscultural content to bridge the cultural gap (Shinn and Eisenman, 2012). China has significantly increased radio programs facing Africa since the 2006 FOCAC (Bailard, 2016). China radio international established its African hub in Nairobi and began local broadcasting in 2006 (Wekesa, 2013). In 2009, CRI built more partnerships with local radio stations and transmitted in six new languages, including Kiswahili (Wu, 2012;Wekesa, 2017;Zhang and Mwangi, 2016). To further strengthen ties with Africa, CRI also broadcasts Mandarin courses and other types of programs on China-Africa cooperation (Wekesa, 2013).

Like traditional broadcasting, podcasts contribute to developing China-Africa relations (Alden and Wu, 2021). Podcasts open up a different news environment, which provides a unique distribution platform for oral audio content with episodic, downloadable, and streamable characteristics (Chan-Olmsted and Wang, 2022;Park, 2017). Podcasts allow anyone to produce and obtain content anywhere and anytime, further enriching information dissemination channels (Rime et al., 2022). Technically, a podcast is a digital audio file (e.g., MP3) delivered via Really Simple Syndication (RSS) to an Internetconnected computer or portable media player (Bottomley, 2015). In the early 1990s, Internet pioneers stumbled upon the innovation of RSS feeds. This digital digest allowed users to subscribe to continuous content on the Web, some of which was audio (Story, 2022). Referred to iPod and broadcast, Guardian journalist Ben Hammersly named the new medium "podcast," and the term was widely accepted (Story, 2022). The podcast originated in 2005 and became popular in 2015 due to the development of new media. The introduction of iTunes 4.9 also made searching and subscribing to podcasts easier (Bottomley, 2015). Podcasts have become an attractive and widely used audio platform (Chan-Olmsted and Wang, 2022) and a widespread cultural phenomenon worldwide (Sullivan, 2019).

From a production and technological perspective, podcasts fall somewhere between radio and social media (Soto-Vásquez et al., 2022). Compared to relatively linear and standardized regular/online radio, podcasts are on-demand and with more content styles (Chan-Olmsted and Wang, 2022), emphasizing the private and personalized experience (Craig et al., 2023;Perks and Turner, 2019;Sharon and John, 2019). Audiences can obtain more diverse podcast information (Perks and Turner, 2019). Besides, audiences usually listen to the radio outdoors but use podcasts at home (Chan-Olmsted and Wang, 2022). What makes podcasting a unique form of audio media is the range of practices and cultural meanings that are thoroughly intertwined with technologies of distribution, organization, and consumption (Chan-Olmsted and Wang, 2022).

International public broadcasters like BBC, NPR, CBC, and ABC favor adopting and launching podcasts (Murray, 2009). China radio international also tried to change its approach to international broadcasting to produce more appealing content to the African public (Bailard, 2016). For example, CRI launched podcasts serving English, Japanese, Korean, and Chinese audiences in July 2006 (Hong, 2009). The China-Africa Project has produced regular podcasts since 2010 (Royston, 2023). In 2018, China Central Television (CCTV), China National Radio (CNR), and China Radio International (CRI) were merged to form the China Media Group (CMG), and the call sign of CRI is still reserved. CMG launched China Plus in 2018, an English Global podcast platform that disseminates various types of podcast content internationally, such as "News & Business," "Society & Culture," "Arts & Music," "Education," and "Audiobooks" (Yan, 2021). As the official English website of CRI, China Plus planned and launched two Africa-themed podcasts in 2021: China Africa Talk and Faces of Africa. Podcasts provide a platform for interaction between China and Africa and a window into China-Africa relations for the global audience. Studies have examined the significant role of podcasts in shaping international discourse on China-Africa relations (Van Staden, 2014). However, how the podcast presents China-Africa relations and shapes the international discourse on the issue still deserves further exploration.

sections.header_norm: media framing and china africa relations
paragraphs: Framing is one of the most fertile topics for journalism and mass communication studies (Aalberg et al., 2012), and scholars have different understandings of framing. Goffman (1974) defined framing as a method people use to categorize and explain the information they come across daily. According to Gitlin (1980), frames are selection, emphasis, and presentation principles made up of subtle notions about what occurs, what matters, and what exists. Entman (1993) highlighted selection and salience as the key framing components, which promote a specific issue definition, causal interpretation, moral assessment, and/or treatment recommendation for the item presented. Framing needs to pick up specific characteristics of a perceived reality and make them stand out more in a context (Entman, 1993). The process of a communication source, such as a news organization, defining and developing a public controversy or political problem is framing (Nelson et al., 1997). Chong and Druckman (2007) defined framing as the process by which people construct a particular conception of an issue or change how they view it. Tuchman (1978) introduced the notion of frames in communication research, which has roots in anthropology and cognitive psychology (Van Gorp, 2005). The news frame is one of the most significant aspects of a news story (McLeod and Detenber, 1999). There is no uniform news frame concept, but many adopted definitions indicate similar characteristics (Semetko and Valkenburg, 2000). News frames are conceptual tools relied on by individuals and media to disseminate, understand, and evaluate information (Neuman et al., 1992), setting the parameters for people discussing public issues (Tuchman, 1978). News frames are "persistent selection, emphasis, and exclusion" (Gitlin, 1980). Media stories usually illustrate either a thematic frame (centered on broader social trends) or an episodic frame (centered on specific cases) (Iyengar, 1991). News framing significantly impacts how audiences perceive and interpret an issue (Nelson et al., 1997) and even influences the attitudes and behaviors of audiences (Chong and Druckman, 2007). News framing is a powerful tool for the media to construct discourse and national image and an ideological tool to consolidate soft power (Ju et al., 2023). Scholars found that news framing of Chinese and American media conveys different social and cultural values (Murphy and Vilceanu, 2014). For example, Hong (2013) revealed that the American media tended to construct negative media framing on the Chinese product recall issue, creating a negative distrust image of China among the audience. Ju et al. (2023) used framing analysis to compare how Chinese and American media used specific framing strategies to portray different national images of China's COVID-19 vaccine. They found that the People's Daily (China) tends to shape a fair, cooperative, and trustworthy image of China. In contrast, the New York Times tends to report a selfish, competitive, and untrustworthy image of China (Ju et al., 2023). The Western media deemed China as an "untrustworthy" partner or an "oppressor" of Africa and perceived China-Africa relations as predominately negative (Ojo, 2016). In contrast, Chinese media reported favorable China-Africa relations and focused on a positive image of China through constructive news (Gabore, 2020;Zhang and Matingwina, 2016). Wekesa (2014) analyzed the reports on FOCAC from 2000 to 2012 and found that Chinese leaders were committed to building a China-Africa development framework of friendship, peace, solidarity, and economy. A comparative study of newspaper framing of China-Africa relations showed that newspapers from different countries construct different media framing, such as the "controversy" and "partner" shown in African news framing, "partnership" and "refutation" in Chinese news framing, and "competitor" and "watchfulness" in American news framing (Olorunnisola and Ma, 2013). The coverage of China-Africa relations in African magazines is centered around three thematic framing of "West versus China," "strategic partnership," and "shared history," which is also closely aligned with China-Africa diplomatic policies (Ojo, 2016). Another study of nonmainstream South African newspapers found that the news framing on China-Africa relations in terms of "economic cooperation," "economic vulturism and colonization," and "political interference," which were generally negative (Munoriyarwa and Chibuwe, 2022). Entman (2004) argued that the media and public framing of diplomatic policy derive from the government's framing.

News framing is defined according to specific issues, events, or political factors (Chong and Druckman, 2007). Studies have shown that the news framing chosen for coverage differs between different media forms, such as newspapers versus television (Semetko and Valkenburg, 2000). Besides, the news framing reported at different times can differ even for the same issue (Chong and Druckman, 2007). Research on content analysis of news framing of China-Africa relations has mainly focused on newspapers, magazines, websites, television, and broadcasts (Chong and Druckman, 2007;Matthes, 2009), but studies on new media such as podcasts still need further exploration (Matanji, 2022).

Framing is one of the most fertile topics for journalism and mass communication studies (Aalberg et al., 2012), and scholars have different understandings of framing. Goffman (1974) defined framing as a method people use to categorize and explain the information they come across daily. According to Gitlin (1980), frames are selection, emphasis, and presentation principles made up of subtle notions about what occurs, what matters, and what exists. Entman (1993) highlighted selection and salience as the key framing components, which promote a specific issue definition, causal interpretation, moral assessment, and/or treatment recommendation for the item presented. Framing needs to pick up specific characteristics of a perceived reality and make them stand out more in a context (Entman, 1993). The process of a communication source, such as a news organization, defining and developing a public controversy or political problem is framing (Nelson et al., 1997). Chong and Druckman (2007) defined framing as the process by which people construct a particular conception of an issue or change how they view it. Tuchman (1978) introduced the notion of frames in communication research, which has roots in anthropology and cognitive psychology (Van Gorp, 2005). The news frame is one of the most significant aspects of a news story (McLeod and Detenber, 1999). There is no uniform news frame concept, but many adopted definitions indicate similar characteristics (Semetko and Valkenburg, 2000). News frames are conceptual tools relied on by individuals and media to disseminate, understand, and evaluate information (Neuman et al., 1992), setting the parameters for people discussing public issues (Tuchman, 1978). News frames are "persistent selection, emphasis, and exclusion" (Gitlin, 1980). Media stories usually illustrate either a thematic frame (centered on broader social trends) or an episodic frame (centered on specific cases) (Iyengar, 1991). News framing significantly impacts how audiences perceive and interpret an issue (Nelson et al., 1997) and even influences the attitudes and behaviors of audiences (Chong and Druckman, 2007). News framing is a powerful tool for the media to construct discourse and national image and an ideological tool to consolidate soft power (Ju et al., 2023). Scholars found that news framing of Chinese and American media conveys different social and cultural values (Murphy and Vilceanu, 2014). sections.header_raw: ANTMN: A computational way for inductive frame analysis
sections.header_norm: antmn a computational way for inductive frame analysis
paragraphs: Regarding the characteristics of framing generation, framing is classified into equivalency and emphasis framing. Equivalency framing requires different but logically equivalent words or phrases to achieve a framing effect, and emphasis framing emphasizes selecting a set of facts or arguments to frame (Cacciatore et al., 2016). Most news reports are about events and issues that do not lend themselves to creating mathematically equivalent frames, and a more appropriate way to understand news framing is by emphasizing them (D'angelo, 2018). Two analysis approaches to news content framing are inductive and deductive (Touri and Koteyko, 2015). The deductive approach predefines specific frames as variables for content analysis to verify the framings shown in the news (Semetko and Valkenburg, 2000;Touri and Koteyko, 2015). Semetko and Valkenburg (2000) conducted a deductive analysis of 2601 newspaper reports and 1522 television news during the 1997 European Summit of Heads of State. The content analysis was based on five news frames: attribution of responsibility, conflict, human interest, economic consequences, and morality. Measured by the frequency of a series of questions, coders were required to answer 'yes' or 'no.' The previously identified frames allow news content to be analyzed systematically. However, relying on manual coding and assessment makes it more challenging to ensure the objectivity of the results (Matthes, 2009). It overlooks essential new frames not included in the predefined categories (Touri and Koteyko, 2015).

The inductive approach starts from a looser framing presupposition and reveals underlying frames from an open perspective (Semetko and Valkenburg, 2000). The inductive approach can be aided by computer-assisted analysis, addressing the shortcomings of the deductive approach (Touri and Koteyko, 2015). Miller (1997) proposed framing mapping and used it to study how the 1996 Republican presidential candidates constructed their images in press releases and how elite newspapers covered them. Miller's framing mapping utilizes computer algorithms to find terms that tend to aggregate with each other and be used exclusively by one stakeholder and is more objective than qualitative methods. Some scholars suggested using topic modeling (DiMaggio et al., 2013) and semantic networks (D'angelo, 2018) for inductive news frame analysis.

Both inductive and deductive approaches are significant in framing analysis and can complement each other in different stages of analysis. Specifically, deductive approaches are critical to developing and applying existing theories, while inductive approaches can help us identify emerging or changing frameworks (Ophir et al., 2023). Walter and Ophir (2019) developed the Analysis of Topic Model Networks (ANTMN), which combines the advantages of inductive and deductive analysis (Ophir et al., 2023). Analysis of topic model networks blends big data analytics, topic modeling, and network analysis to identify and highlight frames from large corpora. Analysis of topic model networks has certain advantages compared to other approaches. First, ANTMN uses an inductive approach to construct topic networks and does not rely on prior knowledge, reducing bias and subjectivity in framing analysis. Second, ANTMN can identify framings easily ignored in previous studies. Third, ANTMN allows the same words to co-exist in various topics and avoids treating separate topics as frames (Walter and Ophir, 2019). From the practical perspective, scholars have applied ANTMN to research work of framing studies, showing good applicability of this method (Ophir et al., 2023;Walter and Ophir, 2019). Analysis of topic model networks provides an approach for this study to analyze the framing of China-Africa relations in podcasts.
Hence, we proposed the following research questions:
RQ1. What kind of media framework does the podcast China Africa Talk use to present China-Africa relations?
RQ2. How does the media framework presented in China Africa Talk reflect China's concept of "building a community of destiny between China and Africa"?
As a new form of audio media, how does China Africa Talk contribute to presenting China-Africa relations from the perspective of historical media development?

sections.paper_id: Zhao et al. - 2024 - Building a shared future between China and Africa A framing analysis of China Radio International's
Zhao et al. - 2024 - Building a shared future between China and Africa A framing analysis of China Radio International's
paragraphs: This study targeted China Africa Talk for research, an English news podcast programmed by China Radio International (CRI) in 2021. CRI has launched two podcasts related to Africa: China Africa Talk and Faces of Africa. China Africa Talk is a news podcast introducing what is happening in Africa and China to audiences. Faces of Africa only tells the stories about people in Africa through audio documentaries. Given that this study is concerned with China-Africa relations, China Africa Talk is better to be the research sample. The podcast airs once a week for around 15 to 20 minutes and focuses on news happening between China and Africa, leaving comments and expressing opinions in interviews. (China Africa Talk, 2023). We collected all episodes (1 st to 36 th ) released before the start of working on this study, which is from 29th October 2021 to 2nd July 2022. The hosts of the China-Africa Dialogue are from Africa. Among the collected 36 episodes, 30 guests are from Africa and 26 from China, with 11 episodes inviting Chinese and African guests to share and discuss online.

paragraphs: This study used Analysis of Topic Model Networks (ANTMN), an Inductive Mixedmethod Computational Approach proposed by Walter and Ophir (2019), to conduct the frame analysis of China Africa Talk. First, we identified frame elements through topic modeling. Topics can be operationalized as frame elements, extracted directly from the text without being assumed. Then, we used topics as nodes to construct a network structure reflecting the topic relationships according to the co-occurrence of topics. Last, we utilized community detection techniques to cluster the co-occurrence topics into coherent frame packages (Ophir et al., 2023). Since ANTMN mainly deals with textual documents and the research sample is audio, we converted the English audio in the podcasts to English documents before the framework analysis. In this study, two researchers with advanced English proficiency were hired to convert, and the final textual content was determined after a two-by-two comparison. Then, we deleted the greetings, modal particles, and other topic-irrelative content and finally obtained 123,846 English words for frame analysis.

In this study, two researchers with advanced English proficiency were hired to convert, and the final textual content was determined after a two-by-two comparison. Then, we deleted the greetings, modal particles, and other topic-irrelative content and finally obtained 123,846 English words for frame analysis.

First, we identified frame elements through topic modeling. Topics can be operationalized as frame elements, extracted directly from the text without being assumed. Then, we used topics as nodes to construct a network structure reflecting the topic relationships according to the co-occurrence of topics.

paragraphs: Topic modeling is a natural language processing approach that characterizes and models texts by extracting appropriate topics and feature words. Topic modeling can reveal the hidden structures (Barbieri et al., 2013) and underlying semantics (Hofmann, 2001) in large text corpora, thus further analyzing the topics of texts.

This study utilized Latent Dirichlet allocation (LDA) complemented by Gibbs Sampling, employing specialized packages for R, such as topic models for LDA implementation and ldatuning for optimal topic number determination (Debnath and Bardhan, 2020). Based on the bag of words, LDA considers documents and words interchangeable, ignores the order of words in documents and the order of documents in the corpus, and thus transforms textual information into digital information that can be easily modeled (Blei et al., 2003). Latent Dirichlet allocation has been widely used for media text analysis, including social media such as Sina Weibo and Twitter (Wang et al., 2020) and traditional news reports (Ophir et al., 2023).

Based on the guidelines suggested by Maier et al. (2018), this study preprocessed the texts firstly, including the removal of stop-words, conversion of capital letters to lowercase, and removal of words that occur in more than 99% of the documents, or less than 0.5% (in this order). This study did not lexicalize or stem words regarding the potential negative impact on topic stability (Ophir et al., 2023;Walter and Ophir, 2019). Regarding the number of topics, this study used a stepwise approach of adding two topics to evaluate a range of models containing 10-100 topics (Ophir et al., 2023) and selected a model containing 29 topics based on four fit metrics (see Appendix). Then, this study followed Walter and Ophir's (2019) methodology and examined three categories of information: the words that are the highest loaded in each topic, the words that are both prevalent and exclusive to each topic, and the full documents that are most representative of each topic.

paragraphs: This study created a network using a theta matrix, in which columns represent individual topics and rows represent documents. It computed the pairwise cosine similarity between topics based on their co-occurrence in documents. Walter and Ophir (2019) used five different detection algorithms to estimate how sensitive the online community structure is, and the results showed similarities for different algorithms. This study referred to Ophir et al. (2023) and chose a Louvain community detection algorithm to identify frames or clusters of topics that tend to co-occur.

sections.paper_id: Zhao et al. - 2024 - Building a shared future between China and Africa A framing analysis of China Radio International's
paragraphs: According to the keywords and content analysis, this study defined the first frame as cultural exchange (shown in purple in Figure 1, accounting for 26.67% of the corpus). This framework contains topics including women, opportunity, challenge, youth, university, culture, film, language, and the Olympics, which reflect the importance of cultural exchange between China and Africa. The cultural exchange is a new framework found in this study, which has not been mentioned in previous studies on the media framing of China-Africa relations (Munoriyarwa and Chibuwe, 2022;Ojo, 2016;Wekesa, 2014). This finding also reflects the difference in content between podcasts and traditional media.

Cultural exchanges have been a prominent feature of China-Africa social relations for over half a century and have laid the foundation for cooperation in the political, economic, media, and educational fields. Under the framework of FOCAC, cultural exchange has become formalized (Shinn and Eisenman, 2012) and increasingly diversified as their cooperation deepens. For example, the 18 th March 2022 episode discussed how Chinese TV programs cross the cultural divide and attract African audiences. The 25 th March 2022 episode discussed the first collaborative film between China and Zimbabwe, which starred African and Chinese actors. Cultural differences between China and Africa are also explored within the framework of cultural exchanges, including the history of and differences in the development of Chinese and African languages and the differences between Chinese and African women's views on money. In addition, the 'youth' and 'Olympics' topics indicate that the podcast follows current events and focuses on cooperation between China and Africa. For instance, the podcast produced three episodes related to the 2022 Beijing Winter Olympics. The episodes introduced the volunteerism of Chinese and African young volunteers, the sportsmanship of African athletes, and the construction and development of African winter sports programs. This frame also concerns youth exchanges between China and Africa. For example, the podcast program invited the China-Africa Leadership Association members to share their stories in China. This content aligns with cultural co-existence under building the China-Africa Community of Shared Future. China advocates "promoting exchanges, mutual understanding and co-existence between Chinese and African civilizations, expanding exchanges and communication in culture and art, education and sports, think tanks and media, women and youth, and tightening the emotional ties between the Chinese and African people" (Central People's Government of the People's Republic of China, 2018). Furthermore, this frame explores the opportunities and challenges China and Africa need to seize and face in cultural communications from a macro perspective.

sections.paper_id: Zhao et al. - 2024 - Building a shared future between China and Africa A framing analysis of China Radio International's
paragraphs: This study identifies the second media frame of China-Africa relations as economic cooperation according to qualitative review and analysis (colored orange in Figure 1, accounting for 20.95% of the corpus). The topics contained in this framework include investment, trade, market, product, economy, and business. Multilateral trade and investment cooperation can date back to the 2000 FOCAC (Matanji, 2022). As previous studies have identified (Munoriyarwa and Chibuwe, 2022;Wekesa, 2014), the economic cooperation frame demonstrated in the podcast shows that economic exchanges still play a crucial role in China-Africa communication and development.

Chinese media traditionally spotlights China's involvement in trade, investment, and African infrastructure (Farooq et al., 2018). However, this study's economic cooperation frame emphasizes reciprocal economic exchanges between China and Africa. China Africa Talk focuses on African commodities entering the Chinese market and collaborating in new economic sectors. The 19 th November 2021 and 15 th April 2022 episodes highlighted the introduction and growing popularity of African dry chili and South African wine in China. The 13 th May 2022 episode explored live e-commerce as a novel driver for China-Africa trade, including insights from young African entrepreneurs in China. The 11 th February 2022 episode also featured Africans in China discussing their development of the China-Africa E-Commerce Platform and their support from the Chinese government.

sections.paper_id: Zhao et al. - 2024 - Building a shared future between China and Africa A framing analysis of China Radio International's
paragraphs: The third frame encompasses food security, climate change, agriculture poverty, infrastructure, and the Belt and Road Initiative (BRI). These issues, pivotal in China, Africa, and globally, are linked to sustainable development in developing nations. Accordingly, this study categorizes this frame as sustainable development (represented in blue in Figure 1, comprising 23.81% of the corpus). This sustainable development frame, a novel media frame about China-Africa relations identified in this research, has not been highlighted in previous studies. It aligns with building a China-Africa Community of Shared Future, advocating for a green, low-carbon, sustainable development approach, and leveraging opportunities from the Belt and Road initiative (Central People's Government of the People's Republic of China, 2018). This frame highlights the sustainable development challenges faced by China and Africa and proposes recommendations to tackle these challenges and promote mutual future development.

The 29 th April 2022 episode featured experts from China and Kenya on climate change and environmental policy, focusing on how both regions address these challenges. It aligns with the vision to "establish a new era of China-Africa strategic partnership to address climate change" (MFA, 2021). In addition to climate change, the 17 th June 2022 episode discussed food insecurity, bringing together experts in agriculture and economics from China and Africa to explore long-term cooperative solutions.

Belt and road initiative is essential for China and Africa to achieve sustainable development under this frame. Recognized globally for promoting trade, investment, and infrastructure development (Apirakvanalee and Zhai, 2023), the BRI is central to the China-Africa Cooperation Vision 2035 (Ministry of Commerce of China, 2021), aiming to bolster sustainable development. The podcast also delved into BRI's role in global infrastructure and sustainable trade. The 11 th March 2022 episode invited Ethiopia's Ambassador to China to explain Ethiopia's policies and decisions in anticipation of further cooperation with China under the BRI framework. Furthermore, the 14 th January 2022 episode examined a significant agreement between China and Kenya, enhancing exports of Kenyan agricultural and fishery products to China, with insights from Peter Biwott on its impact on Kenya's agricultural development.

sections.paper_id: Zhao et al. - 2024 - Building a shared future between China and Africa A framing analysis of China Radio International's
paragraphs: The strategic cooperation frame (28.57%, colored in green in Figure 1) includes partnership, government, policy, relationship, cooperation, and pandemic topics. Previous studies have found relevant frames, such as 'strategic partnership' (Ojo, 2016) and 'friendship' (Wekesa, 2014). China-Africa partnership and strategic cooperation have been central frames of Chinese media coverage of China-Africa relations (Olorunnisola and Ma, 2013;Ojo, 2016). The frame content is oriented to political cooperation, including the long-term cooperation and development of China-Africa relations in the regular and non-conventional periods (e.g., .

The strategic cooperation in the regular period introduced the events between China and Africa, such as the 8 th Ministerial Conference of FOCAC (26 th November 2021 episode). They invited the guests to review the significant events in China-Africa relations in 2021 and discussed the implementation of the last FOCAC summit and the expectations for the upcoming forum. The first episode of 2022 reported the Foreign Minister of China visiting Africa, indicating the deepening of China-Africa strategic cooperation in the new year.

During the COVID-19 pandemic, the podcast focused on how China and Africa could strengthen their strategic cooperation when facing health challenges. The 21 st January 2022 episode mentioned China donating vaccines to Zimbabwe. The 5 th November 2021 episode discussed the use and development of traditional medicine and invited a medical expert to share advice on improving people's mental health during the COVID-19 pandemic. The content under the strategic cooperation echoes Xi Jinping's advocacy of building a China-Africa community with a shared future, such as insisting on unity in the fight against the epidemic and deepening pragmatic cooperation (Ministry of Foreign Affairs of the People's Republic of China, 2021).

sections.paper_id: Zhao et al. - 2024 - Building a shared future between China and Africa A framing analysis of China Radio International's
paragraphs: News framing: The content and development of China's diplomatic policy to Africa

This study provides a complementary analysis of media framing in the context of China-Africa relations as depicted in podcasts. Although China and Africa have a long history of cooperation and diplomatic relations and deep-rooted engagement in politics, economics, and culture (Tu, 2008), exploring China-Africa relations is relatively fresh in journalism and communication (Ojo, 2020). The media framing studies on China-Africa relations are few and mainly focused on traditional media (Munoriyarwa and Chibuwe, 2022;Ojo, 2016;Wekesa, 2014).

This study used ANTMN to analyze the CRI podcast program China Africa Talk and identify four media frames on China-Africa relations: cultural exchange, economic cooperation, sustainable development, and strategic cooperation. The four media frames align with China's development philosophy and direction of building a China-Africa community of a shared future. Powerful groups and individuals, including corporations, interests, and governments, can influence professional news organizations (Park, 2017) and play a central role in defining and articulating to the public the history and relationships between nations (Munoriyarwa and Chibuwe, 2022). In China, news media usually act as a mouthpiece for the government (Hong, 2013;Peng, 2017), and news podcasts also serve the national development strategies. This study provides insights into understanding how Chinese mainstream media construct China-Africa relations.

Compared to previous research, the economic cooperation framework discovered in this study has different themes and emotional tendencies. A study on framing China-Africa relations in mainstream South African newspapers argued that South African newspapers greatly emphasized elements of economic plunder and colonialism, and their media coverage of China has become increasingly negative and hostile (Munoriyarwa and Chibuwe, 2022). The practices and preferences of news sources may lead to negative frames (Munoriyarwa and Chibuwe, 2022). Still, comparing the frames on China-Africa relations between China and Africa examined the critical role of news media in constructing the state relation frames (Marsh, 2016) and the role of news framing in influencing people's perceptions of state relations (Ojo, 2016). The differences indicate the constructive nature of media frames that selectively report on different positions and communication purposes, which in turn constructs targeted frames (Pan and Kosicki, 1993).
Podcast: From "broadcast towards Africa" to "talk in podcasts"

This study provides a reference for studying podcasts in the Asia-Pacific and African regions. As journalism is undergoing controversy and transformation, podcasts have become an essential alternative news release channel (Lindgren, 2023;Park, 2017). Although producing and consuming news podcasts in Western countries has driven great attention, there has been little discussion in Asia-Pacific (Sang et al., 2023).

This study identified two news frames: cultural exchange and sustainable development. The findings reflect the difference between podcasts and newspapers, providing a reference for future new media frame research on China-Africa relations. This study argues that the difference can be explained from two aspects. On the one hand, podcasts unfold in a dialogue format, which is more contagious and engaging (Lindgren, 2023). The guests from China and Africa contribute to the voice exchanges on the same platform (China Plus, 2023). The strategy of employing African hosts for China Africa Talk brought a closer relationship between the program and Africa. As an intersection of media roles and storytellers, hosts build an emotional connection with the audience (Soto-Vásquez et al., 2022), further facilitating exchanges and explorations between the two sides in culture and sustainable development.

On the other hand, podcasts can reach a broader and younger group of audiences than traditional media (Sang et al., 2023). Thus, the content of China Africa Talk focuses more on youth's preferences within the framework of building a China-Africa community of shared future. The government can release non-propaganda and non-political content, such as entertainment stories, lifestyle advice, and practical information, as people are more inclined to consume non-political content (Lu and Pan, 2021).

In conclusion, CRI demonstrated its transformation and adaptability in Internet development by launching podcasts on China-Africa themes. The significance of podcasts in international communication is not only related to the changes in technology, communication presentation, and communication approach but also reflected in the shift from "broadcasting toward Africa" to "talk in podcasts." These changes offer new interpretations of podcasts as a tool for public diplomacy.

This study has certain limitations. It aims to explore the news framing of China-Africa relations as constructed in China Africa Talk, while the intention behind launching the news podcast is not the focus of the investigation. Besides, this study only focuses on the podcast text. The data on the audiences of CRI's podcasts are unavailable through public channels. Thus, the audiences' attitudes towards the podcast and the communication effect of the podcast are not carried out in this study due to limited space. However, the perspective of audiences can be further explored in the future.
